narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dreams despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at patterns from past students—like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated your risk of failing at about 57%, which crossed the threshold for denial. This prediction was driven mostly by two key factors in your profile: your plan to attend part-time and your Year 3 school decile ranking. Being part-time stood out as the biggest influence pushing the model toward predicting failure, since most successful students in the data were full-time (over 90% of them), and part-time paths have historically correlated with tougher odds on the bar. Your Year 3 decile of 3, below the typical 5.5 average, also nudged the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a dip in performance compared to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 and LSAT score of 27—while under the averages of 3.2 and 36.7—still pulled somewhat in the direction of passing, as did your Year 1 decile of 4 and middle family income bracket, showing strengths that the model recognized. Overall, your profile sits below typical successful applicants in these academic and commitment markers, which is why the balance tipped against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many factors like personal responsibilities can shape these numbers, especially for women of color navigating systemic hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades to improve that Year 3 decile, or exploring full-time options if feasible, as these could shift future predictions in your favor. You might also seek programs with holistic reviews or bar prep support tailored to part-time students. Keep pushing; your story is far from over, and with targeted improvements, doors will open. We're rooting for you.
